question: What is a gene?
answer: A section of DNA that codes for one protein
accept: a part of DNA that carries the instructions to make a protein
a length of DNA coding for a protein
misconceptions: a gene is the same thing as a chromosome
a gene is a long thread of DNA
a gene is the whole nucleus

question: What is the twisted-ladder shape of a DNA molecule called?
answer: Double helix
distractors: a chromosome is a coiled thread of DNA, not the shape of the molecule itself
a gene is a section of DNA that codes for a protein, not a shape
the nucleus is the part of the cell that holds the DNA, not the shape of DNA
misconceptions: the shape is called a chromosome
the shape is just a 'twisted ladder' with no scientific name

question: How many chromosomes are found in most human body cells?
working: Most human body cells have 46 chromosomes, arranged in 23 pairs.
misconceptions: 23 (confusing the number of pairs with the total)
92 (doubling the count)
46 pairs rather than 46 chromosomes

question: Human chromosomes are arranged in pairs. How many pairs of chromosomes do most human body cells contain?
answer: 23 pairs
misconceptions: 46 pairs (confusing total chromosomes with pairs)
23 chromosomes rather than 23 pairs
12 pairs

question: In which part of a cell is the genetic material found?
answer: The nucleus
accept: nucleus
misconceptions: the cytoplasm
the cell membrane
the chromosome

question: What is a chromosome?
answer: A long coiled thread of DNA found in the cell nucleus
accept: a long thread of coiled DNA
a long molecule of DNA in the nucleus
misconceptions: a chromosome is the same as a gene
a chromosome is a single protein
a chromosome is the whole nucleus

question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between a gene and a chromosome?
answer: A chromosome is a long thread of DNA, and a gene is a small section of it that codes for a protein
options: A gene and a chromosome are the same thing
A chromosome is a long thread of DNA, and a gene is a small section of it that codes for a protein
A gene is a long thread of DNA, and a chromosome is a small section of it
A chromosome codes for a protein, and a gene holds many chromosomes
distractors: a gene and a chromosome are not the same; a gene is just one section of a chromosome
this reverses the two; the chromosome is the long thread and the gene is the small section
it is the gene that codes for a protein, and a chromosome holds many genes, not the reverse
misconceptions: a gene is the same thing as a chromosome
a chromosome is a section of a gene

question: Describe how genetic information is organised, from the nucleus down to a gene.
answer: The nucleus contains chromosomes
chromosomes are made of DNA
a gene is a section of DNA that codes for a protein
accept: nucleus holds chromosomes, which are made of DNA, and a gene is a part of the DNA
misconceptions: genes contain chromosomes
DNA is made of genes which are made of chromosomes
the nucleus is inside the chromosome

question: What do genes code for?
answer: Proteins
accept: a protein
the instructions to make a protein
misconceptions: genes code for chromosomes
genes code for whole characteristics directly with no protein involved
genes code for DNA

question: What does the term heredity mean?
answer: Passing genetic information from one generation to the next
accept: the transmission of genetic information from parents to offspring
misconceptions: heredity means becoming better adapted over your lifetime
heredity means all characteristics are identical to one parent
heredity means traits from parents blend together

question: Explain why half of a child's genetic information comes from their mother and half from their father.
answer: The ovum carries 23 chromosomes / half the genetic information
the sperm carries 23 chromosomes / the other half
they combine at fertilisation to form a zygote with a complete set
accept: one chromosome of each pair comes from each parent
misconceptions: all genetic information comes from the mother because the ovum is bigger
the characteristics from each parent blend together
the child gets a full set from each parent

question: How many chromosomes are in the nucleus of a human sperm cell?
distractors: 46 is the number in a body cell, not a sperm cell, which carries half
92 would be double a body cell, far too many
2 confuses chromosomes with the number of parents
misconceptions: a sperm cell has 46 chromosomes like a body cell
a sperm cell has no chromosomes

question: What is the full name of the molecule that chromosomes are made of?
answer: DNA
accept: deoxyribonucleic acid
misconceptions: chromosomes are made of protein
chromosomes are made of genes
chromosomes are made of RNA

question: Why is it incorrect to say that a gene and a chromosome are the same thing?
answer: A chromosome is a long coiled thread of DNA
a gene is just one small section of that DNA that codes for a protein
there are many genes on each chromosome
accept: a chromosome contains many genes, so they cannot be the same
misconceptions: a gene is the same thing as a chromosome
a chromosome is a section of a gene
each chromosome holds only one gene

question: Whose X-ray evidence helped Watson and Crick work out the structure of DNA, and what was the key photograph called?
answer: Rosalind Franklin (and Raymond Gosling) produced the X-ray evidence
the key image was called Photograph 51
Watson and Crick used this evidence to show DNA was a double helix
accept: Maurice Wilkins also worked on DNA structure alongside Franklin
misconceptions: Watson and Crick discovered the structure entirely on their own
the photograph proved DNA had three strands
Gregor Mendel took the X-ray photograph

question: Which scientist studied garden peas and suggested there were 'units' of inheritance, now called genes?
answer: Gregor Mendel
distractors: Charles Darwin studied finches and natural selection, not pea inheritance units
Rosalind Franklin produced X-ray images of DNA, not the pea studies
James Watson worked on the structure of DNA, not the garden pea experiments
misconceptions: Darwin discovered units of inheritance
Watson and Crick first proposed units of inheritance

question: Two siblings have the same parents but look different from each other. Explain why.
answer: Each sibling inherits a different combination of genes/DNA from each parent
which characteristics they show depends on which genes they inherit
this process is random
accept: different DNA is inherited from each parent by each sibling
misconceptions: siblings must look identical because they share parents
one sibling is a copy of the mother and the other a copy of the father
the parents' characteristics blend equally in every child

question: Two genes both code for eye-colour pigment but produce different colours. Explain how the genes can do this.
answer: Each gene codes for a different protein
the proteins have different shapes / arrangements of atoms
so they act as different pigments (e.g. brown or blue)
accept: different genes code for differently shaped proteins
misconceptions: a gene directly is the eye colour, with no protein involved
both genes make the same protein
the gene is made of pigment itself

question: What is meant by competition between organisms?
answer: When organisms struggle for the same limited resources
accept: organisms competing for the same resources that are in short supply
misconceptions: competition means organisms choosing to fight
competition means organisms helping each other
competition only happens between animals, never plants

question: Name the three things that animals compete with each other for.
answer: Food
mates
territory
accept: shelter instead of territory
misconceptions: animals compete for sunlight (that is plants)
animals compete for minerals (that is plants)
animals do not compete at all

question: Which list correctly shows what plants compete with each other for?
answer: Sunlight, water, minerals and space
distractors: food, mates and territory is what animals compete for, not plants
mates and shelter apply to animals, and plants do not compete for oxygen
this mixes animal needs (food, mates) with plant needs
misconceptions: plants compete for food and mates like animals
plants do not need to compete because they make their own food

question: What is natural selection?
answer: The process where the best-adapted organisms survive, reproduce and pass on their genes
accept: organisms best suited to their environment survive and pass on their characteristics
misconceptions: organisms choose to adapt themselves so they survive
every organism in a population survives equally
the strongest organism always survives regardless of its environment

question: Explain why a darker-furred bear is less successful than a lighter-furred bear at hunting in the snowy Arctic.
answer: The darker bear is not camouflaged against the white surroundings
so its prey (seals) can see it approaching more easily
so it catches less food and is less successful at surviving
accept: the lighter bear is camouflaged, so it catches more prey
misconceptions: the darker bear can change its fur to lighter so it survives
the darker bear is simply weaker, not poorly camouflaged
fur colour makes no difference to hunting

question: A deer has small antlers and cannot compete well for mates. Which statement is correct?
answer: The deer cannot grow larger antlers; antler size varies naturally and the best-adapted deer compete more successfully
options: The deer will grow larger antlers so it can compete successfully
The deer cannot grow larger antlers; antler size varies naturally and the best-adapted deer compete more successfully
All deer have the same antler size, so competition does not happen
The deer chooses to develop bigger antlers during the mating season
distractors: organisms cannot choose to change themselves; the deer cannot grow larger antlers on demand
there is natural variation in antler size, so deer are not all the same
a deer cannot choose to develop bigger antlers; the variation already exists
misconceptions: an organism can choose to change or adapt itself so it survives better
a deer can grow its antlers longer to win at competing

question: Why do animals compete with each other for mates?
answer: So they have a better chance of reproducing successfully
so they can pass on their genes to future generations
accept: to reproduce and pass on their characteristics to offspring
misconceptions: they compete for mates to choose to become better adapted
they compete for mates to get more food
competing for mates has no link to passing on genes

question: In one area there are very few insects but plenty of fruit. Explain what happens to the populations of insect-eating and fruit-eating finches over many generations.
answer: Insect-eating finches cannot get enough food, so they are less likely to survive and reproduce
fruit-eating finches have plenty of food, so they are more likely to survive, reproduce and pass on their genes
over many generations the fruit-eating population increases and the insect-eating population decreases
accept: the better-adapted (fruit-eating) finches increase while the others decrease
misconceptions: the insect-eating finches change their beaks to eat fruit
the change happens in only one or two generations
the finches choose to adapt to the available food

question: Which scientist studied finches on the Galapagos Islands and proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection?
answer: Charles Darwin
distractors: Gregor Mendel studied inheritance in garden peas, not Galapagos finches
Francis Crick worked on the structure of DNA, not natural selection
Maurice Wilkins worked on DNA structure, not the Galapagos finches
misconceptions: Mendel proposed the theory of evolution
Watson and Crick discovered natural selection

question: A student says a polar bear chose to grow white fur so it could survive in the Arctic. Explain why this is wrong.
answer: Organisms cannot choose to adapt themselves
natural variation in fur colour already exists in the population
the best-camouflaged bears simply survive and reproduce more, passing on their genes
accept: the variation is already there; bears do not decide to change
misconceptions: an organism can choose to change or adapt itself so it survives better
a bear can will its fur to turn white
adaptation happens within a single bear's lifetime by choice

question: What is meant by the term 'variation'?
answer: The differences between individuals in a population
accept: differences between organisms of the same species
differences within a population
misconceptions: differences between different species
characteristics that are the same in everyone
only differences you can see

question: What are the two main causes of variation?
answer: Genes and the environment
options: Only the genes inherited from parents
distractors: a common error is thinking all variation is inherited through genes, but some is caused by the environment
diet and exercise are only environmental factors and miss genes entirely
organisms cannot choose their characteristics
misconceptions: all variation is inherited from your parents through your genes
organisms choose their own characteristics

question: Give two examples of variation that is caused by your genes.
answer: natural hair colour
eye colour (or height, or blood group, or whether ear lobes are attached)
accept: natural/inherited hair colour
eye colour
height
blood group
attached or unattached ear lobes
misconceptions: dyed hair colour
scars
a suntan
a chosen hairstyle

question: Give two examples of variation that is caused by the environment.
answer: scars
dyed hair colour (or hairstyle, tattoos, or a suntan)
accept: scars
dyed hair
hairstyle
tattoos
suntan
misconceptions: natural hair colour
eye colour
blood group
attached ear lobes

question: A person has a scar on their arm. Explain why this is an example of variation caused by the environment and not by their genes.
answer: a scar is the result of something that happened to them, such as an injury
it is not coded for by the genes inherited from their parents
so it cannot be passed on to their offspring
accept: caused by an injury/event, not inherited
not passed on to children because genes do not cause it
misconceptions: scars are inherited from parents
the scar will be passed on to their children
genes cause the scar

question: Most variation in humans is caused by a combination of genes and the environment. Using height as an example, explain how both can affect it.
answer: height is controlled by the genes inherited from parents
but it can also be affected by the environment, such as diet
so the final height depends on both genes and environment together
accept: genes set the potential height and diet/environment affects how much of it is reached
both inherited genes and factors like diet influence height
misconceptions: height is caused only by genes
height is caused only by diet
you can choose how tall you grow

question: An animal has long fur. Is this variation most likely caused by genes or the environment?
answer: Genes
distractors: fur length is an inherited characteristic, not something the surroundings create on their own
animals cannot choose to grow longer fur
an injury would not cause longer fur to grow
misconceptions: the animal chose to grow long fur to survive
long fur is caused only by the cold environment

question: A green plant suddenly develops yellow leaves after being kept in the dark. Is this change caused by genes or the environment? Give a reason.
answer: the environment
because the change happened due to the conditions the plant was kept in, not the genes it inherited
accept: environment - caused by lack of light/conditions, not inherited genes
misconceptions: genes, because the plant inherited yellow leaves
the plant chose to change its leaf colour

question: Holly leaves are naturally smooth. After an animal eats some leaves, the new leaves grow back spiky. Is this change caused by genes or the environment?
answer: the environment
because the spiky leaves grew as a response to being eaten, not from the genes the plant inherited
accept: environment - it is a response to being eaten, not an inherited change
misconceptions: genes, because the plant inherited spiky leaves
the plant chose to grow spiky leaves to protect itself

question: Why are offspring not 100% like either of their parents?
answer: they get half their DNA from their mother
and the other half from their father
so they end up with a mixture of both parents' genetic information
accept: half their genes come from each parent, giving a combination
misconceptions: offspring are an exact copy of one parent
offspring blend the parents' features evenly into an average
offspring only inherit from their mother

question: What is continuous variation?
answer: variation where the data for a characteristic can fall anywhere within a range of values
accept: the values form a range rather than separate groups
data can take any value within a range
misconceptions: variation that falls into separate categories
variation caused only by the environment
variation that never changes

question: What is discontinuous variation?
answer: variation where organisms can be placed into separate categories
the values are separate and do not fall within a range
accept: data falls into distinct groups, not a range
separate categories with no in-between values
misconceptions: variation that falls anywhere within a range
variation caused only by the environment
variation that is the same in everyone

question: A group of people are surveyed for their eye colour. What type of variation does this show?
answer: Discontinuous variation
distractors: eye colour falls into separate categories, not a continuous range like height does
eye colour is determined by genes but the data is in categories, not environmental
there are clearly differences, so there is variation
misconceptions: eye colour is continuous because there are many shades
eye colour variation is caused by the environment

question: A group of people are surveyed for their foot length. What type of variation is this showing?
answer: continuous variation
accept: continuous
misconceptions: discontinuous variation
environmental variation
no variation

question: Explain why height in a population shows continuous variation.
answer: because a person's height can be any value within a range
most people fall in the middle of the range, with a few very short or very tall
accept: height takes any value across a range rather than separate categories
misconceptions: height falls into a few separate categories
everyone is either short or tall with nothing in between
height shows discontinuous variation

question: Blood group is an example of discontinuous variation. Explain why, and state what causes this type of variation.
answer: because a person can only be in one of a few separate blood group categories
the values are separate and do not fall within a range
discontinuous variation is usually only caused by your genes
accept: blood groups are distinct categories with no in-between values, caused by genes
misconceptions: blood group can take any value in a range
blood group is caused by the environment
blood group shows continuous variation

question: What type of graph is used to display discontinuous variation, such as blood group?
answer: a bar chart
accept: bar chart
bar graph
misconceptions: a line graph
a smooth curve
a scatter graph

question: Describe the shape of the curve produced when the heights of many people are plotted.
answer: most people fall in the middle of the range of heights
fewer people have very low or very high heights at either end
accept: a hump/curve with most people in the middle and few at each extreme
misconceptions: the bars are all equal heights
the line goes straight up
there is no pattern

question: On a bar chart showing the percentage of the population with each blood group, why is there no overall trend or pattern in the bars?
answer: because a person can be in any one of the blood group categories, so the bars do not follow a pattern
accept: the categories are independent, so there is no rising or falling trend
misconceptions: the bars always increase from left to right
the most common blood group is always the same height as the others

question: Roughly what percentage of their DNA do humans share with each other?
distractors: 50% is how much DNA you inherit from each individual parent, not how much humans share overall
75% is not a figure given for shared human DNA
humans share almost all their DNA, far more than 10%
misconceptions: humans share only half their DNA with each other
humans are mostly genetically different from one another

question: Despite all the differences we can see between people, why is it true to say humans are also very similar?
answer: because humans share 99.9% of their DNA with each other
so most of our genetic information is the same
accept: we share almost all of our DNA, only a tiny amount differs
misconceptions: humans share only half their DNA
the visible differences mean humans are mostly different

question: Which of these is an example of variation that you CANNOT easily see?
answer: Blood group
distractors: hair colour is a visible difference
height is something you can observe
eye colour is easily seen
misconceptions: all variation can be seen by looking at a person
blood group is visible on the outside

question: Variation in characteristics we cannot see, such as blood group, is caused by what?
answer: our DNA (our genes)
accept: genes
DNA
inherited genetic information
misconceptions: the environment
diet
choices we make

question: A person catches chickenpox, which is caused by a virus. Is this an example of variation caused by genes? Explain your answer.
answer: no
because chickenpox is caused by a virus from the environment, not by genes being passed on
accept: no - it is caused by a virus, an environmental factor, not inherited genes
misconceptions: yes, chickenpox is inherited from parents
yes, it is caused by genes

question: Give one key difference between continuous and discontinuous variation.
answer: continuous variation falls anywhere within a range of values
whereas discontinuous variation falls into separate categories
accept: continuous = a range; discontinuous = distinct separate groups
misconceptions: they are the same thing
continuous variation falls into categories and discontinuous into a range
the difference is only the type of graph used

question: In a class of 30 pupils, blood groups are recorded. 15 are group O, 9 are group A, 4 are group B and the rest are group AB. How many pupils have blood group AB?
misconceptions: adding the groups instead of subtracting
forgetting one of the blood groups

question: A survey of 200 people records their eye colour. If 130 have brown eyes and 45 have blue eyes, how many have a different eye colour?
misconceptions: adding 130 and 45 instead of subtracting from 200
subtracting only one value from the total

question: In a class practical, pupils measure each other's hand width and record the results. What type of variation will the hand width data show?
answer: Continuous variation, because it can be any value in a range
options: Continuous variation, because it can be any value in a range
Discontinuous variation, because the values fall into separate categories
Variation caused only by the environment
distractors: hand width is not split into separate categories, so it is not discontinuous
hand width is largely inherited, not purely environmental
there will be a range of different hand widths, so there is variation
misconceptions: hand width falls into separate categories like blood group
hand width is caused only by the environment
